vulnerabilities.title: Session call signatures are replayable across wallets sharing session config and nonce state
vulnerabilities.summary: Session signatures are recovered from `keccak256(chainId-or-0, space, nonce, callIdx, hashCall(call))` in `SessionSig.hashCallWithReplayProtection`, which does not include wallet identity. `SessionSig.recoverSignature` uses this wallet-agnostic hash to recover each `sessionSigner`, and `SessionManager.recoverSapientSignature` then validates permissions and usage against `msg.sender` (the current wallet). As a result, the exact same session signature can authorize execution on a different wallet

description.desc: `hashCallWithReplayProtection` omits wallet/domain binding, so recovered session signatures are valid for any wallet with matching payload nonce/space and session configuration.

vulnerabilities.impact: Cross-wallet replay can duplicate authorized session actions across multiple wallets, causing unintended token/ETH transfers and direct loss of assets from replayed wallets.
vulnerabilities.proof_of_concept: 1. Victim signs and submits a valid session payload on wallet A (signature is public in calldata/mempool). 2. Attacker identifies wallet B that uses the same session configuration and has matching `space/nonce` (and compatible cumulative usage counters). 3. Attacker replays the same packed payload and session signature to wallet B. 4. Signature recovery succeeds again because call hashes are identical across wallets, and wallet B executes unauthorized transfers/calls.
vulnerabilities.remediation: Bind session call signatures to wallet domain. Include the wallet address (and, if needed, parent-wallet context) in `hashCallWithReplayProtection`, or switch this verification path to compact sapient signing bound to the parent `_opHash`.

vulnerabilities.title: Empty chained signature (`0x01`) bypasses auth on Stage1 wallet deployed at zero imageHash
vulnerabilities.summary: When signature flag bit0 is set, `BaseSig.recover` routes to `recoverChained`. If the chained body is empty (e.g. signature exactly `0x01`), the loop in `recoverChained` is skipped and the function returns zero-initialized values. `BaseAuth.signatureValidation` only rejects when `weight < threshold`; with `(0,0)` this check passes. Validation then calls `_isValidImage(imageHash)`. In Stage1, `_isValidImage` checks CREATE2 address derivation from `FACTORY`, `INIT_CODE_HASH`, and `_imageHash` (use

description.desc: `recoverChained` does not enforce a minimum segment count; an empty chained body returns default `(threshold=0, weight=0, imageHash=0)` which passes `weight < threshold` checks.

vulnerabilities.impact: The affected Stage1 zero-salt wallet is effectively permissionless: any attacker can execute arbitrary calls and steal all wallet assets without possessing any signer key.
vulnerabilities.proof_of_concept: 1. A Stage1 wallet exists at the deterministic CREATE2 address for salt `bytes32(0)` (deployment is allowed by `Factory.deploy`). 2. Attacker crafts any transfer/takeover payload and submits `execute(payload, 0x01)`. 3. Chained recovery returns zero values; signature validation accepts it; Stage1 image check passes for zero-salt wallet. 4. Wallet executes attacker-controlled calls (drain assets and/or self-call `updateImageHash` to attacker-controlled config).
vulnerabilities.remediation: Reject empty chained signatures by requiring at least one chained segment in `recoverChained`. Additionally harden deployment/validation paths by disallowing zero imageHash salts for Stage1 wallets.
